questions.question_text: Under Maryland's current law (effective October 2024), what is the maximum security deposit a landlord may collect?
questions.explanation: Under the Renters' Rights and Stabilization Act (RRSA) effective October 2024, Maryland limits security deposits to one month's rent. The previous limit was two months' rent. A narrow exception allows up to two months' rent only when the tenant has qualified for utility assistance through the Department of Human Services, the lease requires utility payments to be made directly to the landlord, and both parties agree in writing (RP §8-203(b)).

questions.question_text: A Maryland landlord holds a tenant's $1,500 security deposit for eight months. What interest must the landlord pay when returning the deposit?
questions.correct_answer: Greater of the 1-year Treasury rate or 1.5%
questions.explanation: Because the deposit is at least $50 and was held six months or longer, RP §8-203(e) requires simple interest at the daily U.S. Treasury yield curve rate for 1 year (as of the first business day of the year) or 1.5% per year, whichever is greater, accruing in monthly installments (not compounded). Maryland provides an online calculator to compute the exact amount.

questions.question_text: A Maryland tenant moves out and the landlord withholds the entire security deposit without providing itemized deductions. A court finds the withholding was unjustified. What penalty may the landlord face?
questions.correct_answer: Up to treble damages plus attorney fees
questions.explanation: Maryland law permits a tenant to recover up to three times the wrongfully withheld amount plus reasonable attorney's fees (RP §8-203(e)(4)). This penalty is discretionary with the court and encourages compliance with deposit return requirements.

questions.question_text: Within what timeframe must a Maryland landlord return a security deposit after a tenant moves out?
questions.explanation: Maryland landlords must return the security deposit within 45 days of the tenant vacating, accompanied by itemized deductions for any amounts withheld. Failure to do so can result in treble damages.

questions.question_text: A Maryland landlord collects a security deposit but does not place it in an escrow account or provide a written receipt. Which violations has the landlord committed?
distractors: No violation if deposit is returned timely
questions.explanation: Maryland landlords must deposit a security deposit within 30 days of receipt into a Maryland-branch, federally insured account devoted exclusively to security deposits (RP §8-203(d)), and must give the tenant a written receipt — which may be included in the lease — describing the tenant's inspection and refund rights (RP §8-203(c), §8-203.1; $25 penalty for failure). Failing to do both constitutes two separate violations.

questions.question_text: Under Maryland law, which type of lease must be in writing?
questions.explanation: Maryland requires leases with a term exceeding one year to be in writing. Shorter leases may be oral, though written leases are always recommended for clarity and enforceability.

questions.question_text: A Maryland tenant has not paid rent for two weeks. The landlord changes the locks while the tenant is at work. Is this action legal?
distractors: Yes, if the landlord gives verbal notice
Only if the tenant has no children
questions.explanation: Self-help evictions, including changing locks or removing a tenant's possessions, are illegal in Maryland regardless of the circumstances. Landlords must use the court eviction process even for non-payment of rent.

questions.question_text: A Maryland tenant's lease violation involves non-rent issues such as unauthorized pets. What notice must the landlord provide before pursuing eviction?
questions.explanation: For lease breaches other than nonpayment of rent, Maryland landlords must give 30 days' written notice (14 days if the tenant's behavior presents a clear and imminent danger of serious harm) before filing (RP §8-402.1). For nonpayment of rent, the landlord must first serve a written notice of intent to file and wait 10 days before filing suit (RP §8-401(c)).

questions.question_text: A Maryland rental unit has a broken furnace in January. The landlord ignores repeated repair requests. What remedy is available to the tenant after following proper procedure?
distractors: Repair the furnace and deduct the cost from rent
questions.explanation: Lack of heat is a serious and substantial threat to life, health, or safety under RP §8-211. After giving the landlord notice and a reasonable opportunity to repair, the tenant may bring a rent escrow action and pay rent into court — or refuse rent and raise the conditions as an affirmative defense, with relief conditioned on paying the rent into court escrow. Maryland law never authorizes unilateral rent withholding, and there is no general repair-and-deduct remedy.

questions.question_text: A Maryland tenant's one-year lease expires, but the tenant stays and the landlord continues accepting monthly rent. What tenancy results?
distractors: A new one-year lease term automatically
questions.explanation: Under RP §8-402, when a landlord consents to a holdover tenant remaining — such as by accepting rent — the tenant becomes a periodic month-to-month tenant (week-to-week if the prior tenancy was week-to-week). To end a month-to-month tenancy, the landlord must give at least 60 days' written notice; only a court can order an eviction.

questions.question_text: Under Maryland's Reduction of Lead Risk in Housing Act, which rental properties must be registered with the Maryland Department of Environment?
distractors: All rental properties regardless of age
Only units with confirmed lead paint
questions.explanation: Maryland requires all pre-1978 rental properties to be registered with the Maryland Department of Environment (MDE). This applies regardless of whether lead paint has been confirmed, as 1978 is when lead paint was banned.

questions.question_text: A Maryland landlord rents out a 1965-built home without obtaining a lead-safe compliance certificate or providing lead paint information to the tenant. A child in the unit is diagnosed with lead poisoning. What liability does the landlord face?
questions.correct_answer: Significant liability for the child's injuries
distractors: No liability if the paint looked intact
Liability limited to medical costs only
A small fine from MDE only
questions.explanation: Maryland has one of the strictest lead paint laws nationally. Landlords who fail to comply with registration, lead-safe certification, and disclosure requirements face significant liability, especially when a child suffers lead poisoning.

questions.question_text: Before renting a pre-1978 property in Maryland, the landlord must ensure the unit meets which standard?
questions.explanation: Maryland requires pre-1978 rental properties to meet lead-safe housing standards before being rented. Landlords must maintain compliance certificates and register with MDE as part of the Reduction of Lead Risk in Housing Act.

questions.question_text: A landlord owns a 1972 rental property in Maryland and has maintained a valid lead compliance certificate. The landlord sells the property. What must the new owner do before continuing to rent?
questions.correct_answer: Obtain their own MDE registration and certificate
distractors: Nothing if the old certificate is current
Wait until the certificate expires to renew
Only notify the tenant of the ownership change
questions.explanation: New owners of affected (pre-1978) rental properties must register with the Maryland Department of the Environment within 30 days after acquisition (Environment §6-812) and renew annually by December 31 — the previous owner's registration does not transfer. Lead-safe compliance must also be satisfied at each change in occupancy.

questions.question_text: Maryland's lead paint law is considered one of the strictest in the nation primarily because it:
distractors: Requires annual lead testing of tenants
Mandates full lead abatement before sale
questions.explanation: Maryland's Reduction of Lead Risk in Housing Act is among the strictest nationally due to the significant liability landlords face for non-compliance, particularly when children suffer lead poisoning. It does not ban pre-1978 rentals outright.
